title: i18n / Language Packs for Vector
topic: Best-practice approach to making every user-visible string in MMFFDev Vector (Next.js 14 App Router + Go chi + Postgres, multi-tenant) translatable, including UI labels, system-emitted text, and gadmin-authored content
summary: next-intl + ICU + Go message keys; phased plan starts with ESLint gate; DB content via sidecar tables, not JSONB.
content:
Contents
  1. 1. Executive Summary
  2. 2. The Problem Space
  3. 3. Comparison of Approaches
  4. 4. Recommended Migration Path for Vector
  5. 5. Vector-Specific Risks
  6. 6. References
  7. 7. Gaps

1. Executive Summary

For Vector's Next.js 14 App Router frontend the recommended runtime is next-intl 4.x: it is the only library in the field designed natively for the App Router and React Server Components, ships an ICU MessageFormat pipeline with compile-time minification (~1 KB runtime), and provides type-safe translation keys generated from the message catalogue. For the Go backend, error and audit strings should be returned as keys + interpolation params (e.g. err.itm.deleted_archived with {title: "..."}) and resolved client-side via the same next-intl messages bundle, with nicksnyder/go-i18n as a fallback for server-rendered emails. For tenant- and gadmin-authored DB content (custom field labels, Page Help bodies, custom field default values), Vector should use sidecar translation tables (<table>_i18n) rather than JSONB name_i18n columns, because Vector already enforces tenant isolation via row-level constraints and needs full-text search per locale. A pairing with Tolgee self-hosted (Apache-2.0) is the recommended translator workflow tool because of its in-context editing, screenshot capture, and JSON/PO export that round-trips with next-intl. The migration is phased over eight stages, starting with a CI lint gate (eslint-plugin-i18next/no-literal-string) so no new hardcoded English can land while the existing strings are extracted in batches.

2. The Problem Space

What "every string translatable" actually means

The phrase hides at least seven distinct categories of string, each with a different lifecycle, owner, and storage location. Bolt-on i18n usually fails because a team translates one or two of these categories and discovers the rest only when a customer asks why half the UI is still English.

CategoryOwnerStorage todayTranslation strategy
Static UI stringsEngineeringJSX literal in .tsx filesExtract to message catalogue keyed by code-relative path
Validation messagesEngineeringInline strings in form/zod handlersSame catalogue, ICU plural support required
Tooltips / aria-labels / placeholdersEngineeringProps on JSX elementsSame catalogue; ESLint must lint attribute strings, not just text nodes
System-generated contentBackendHardcoded English in Go handlers (audit log labels, sprint status names, error messages)Backend returns key + params, frontend resolves; or Go-side resolution for emails/exports
Gadmin-authored contentGlobal adminbody_html columns (Page Help, library release notes)Per-locale row in sidecar table; HTML stored once per locale
Tenant-authored contentPadmin / username / description on user-created entitiesSidecar table optional; tenants may opt out and accept their own input language as canonical
Format-sensitive valuesGeneratedServer-side toISOString(), JS toLocaleString() at random sitesPass raw value (Date / number) through props; format at the leaf via useFormatter()

Cross-cutting axes that affect every category

  • Pluralization. Six CLDR categories (zero, one, two, few, many, other) — Arabic uses all six, Polish uses three (1 / 2-4 / 5+), English uses two. A naive {count} item{count !== 1 ? 's' : ''} pattern is wrong in any non-English locale.
  • Date / number / currency formatting. Locale-dependent (1,234.56 vs 1.234,56 vs '1 234,56). The runtime must hand a raw Date or number to the formatter at the leaf — never pre-stringify in the data layer.
  • Right-to-left languages. Arabic, Hebrew, Persian, Urdu need dir="rtl" on <html> and the entire CSS catalogue audited for physical (left/right) properties — these must become logical (inline-start/inline-end).
  • Postgres collations and full-text search. A single shared english text-search config will not return matches for German, French, or CJK. Per-locale tsvector columns or per-tenant text-search config become necessary.
  • Locale routing. Subpath (/en/...), subdomain (en.vector...), or cookie-only? Each has SEO, caching, and middleware implications.

Why bolt-on i18n is painful

  • String-extraction debt is unbounded. A 2-year-old codebase typically holds 5,000–15,000 user-visible strings buried in JSX, prop defaults, error throws, and template literals. Manual extraction takes weeks; AST-based extraction misses anything constructed at runtime (e.g. `Welcome ${user.name}`).
  • Hardcoded English in DB rows. Once a body_html column has shipped with English content, every existing row must be migrated when the locale column is added. Defaulting to "English in column X is the canonical version" locks Vector into English forever for that content type.
  • String concatenation breaks word order. {verb} {noun} works in English ("Edit Project") but fails in Japanese, where the verb follows the noun. Translators need whole sentences with placeholders, not pieces.
  • JSX inline elements break naive extraction. <p>You have <strong>{count}</strong> items</p> cannot be a single key without a rich-text-aware formatter (next-intl supports this; raw i18next does not without extra plumbing).
  • Server-rendered HTML in DB cannot be re-translated after the fact without re-authoring; machine-translation memory does not survive HTML round-trips well.
  • Auth-gated screens are invisible to translators unless the workflow tool supports in-context editing (Tolgee's differentiator).

3. Comparison of Approaches

Eight approaches are scored across ten criteria, each /10. Higher is better in every case (bundle-size and migration-effort scores are inverted from raw cost). The total is the simple sum (max 100). The recommendation column is a one-sentence verdict for Vector specifically.

Scoring criteria

  • App Router: native support for Next.js 14+ App Router file conventions and i18n/request.ts pattern.
  • SSR / RSC: works inside React Server Components without forcing the page into a client boundary.
  • Bundle: smaller runtime → higher score (next-intl ICU-minified ~1 KB = 10; FormatJS ~40 KB = 4).
  • Type safety: compile-time-checked translation keys and ICU argument types.
  • ICU: supports the full ICU MessageFormat syntax (plurals, gender, ordinals, dates, selectordinals).
  • Workflow: translator workflow features (export/import, translation memory, screenshots, in-context editing) — built-in or via well-trodden integration.
  • DB content: usable for tenant-authored / gadmin-authored DB content, not just static UI.
  • Migration: low friction from Vector's zero-i18n starting state → higher score.
  • Maintenance: stable API, active maintainer, mature release cadence.
  • License / cost: OSS & free-to-self-host → 10; per-seat SaaS → lower.

Comparison table

ApproachApp RouterSSR / RSCBundleType safetyICUWorkflowDB contentMigrationMaintenanceLicense / costTotalRecommendation
next-intl 4.x 10 10 10 9 10 6 4 8 9 10 86 Pick this for the UI runtime. Best App Router + RSC story, native ICU, sub-1 KB compiled runtime, type-safe keys.
i18next + react-i18next 7 7 5 7 6 9 6 7 10 10 74 Strong second; choose only if Vector needs the i18next plugin ecosystem (locize backend, missing-key reporters) more than RSC ergonomics.
next-i18next v16 8 7 5 6 6 9 6 7 9 10 73 Pages-Router heritage now patched for App Router; viable but inherits i18next's non-ICU JSON format and adds a wrapper layer.
LinguiJS 5 6 7 9 9 10 7 3 5 9 10 75 Best DX (compile-time macros), tiny bundle, ICU; loses on App Router maturity and DB-content fit. Excellent for greenfield React, weaker for Vector's mid-life retrofit.
react-intl (FormatJS) 5 4 4 8 10 9 3 5 10 10 68 The original ICU implementation, but has a documented open issue around RSC support; bundle is ~40 KB. Mature, but the wrong shape for Vector's App Router.
Tolgee runtime + SDK 7 7 6 7 8 10 8 6 9 9 77 Strongest translator workflow (Alt-click in-context editing, auto-screenshots) and self-hostable; pair with next-intl rather than replace it. Treat as the "TMS layer," not the runtime.
Phrase / Lokalise / Crowdin SaaS n/a n/a n/a n/a 10 10 7 7 9 3 n/a (workflow-only) Not a runtime. Pair with next-intl if Vector needs CAT/TM SaaS (Crowdin from $25/mo, Lokalise ~$120/mo, Phrase ~$385/mo); Tolgee self-hosted is the cheaper alternative.
Roll-your-own (DB i18n_strings + t() helper) 4 5 9 3 2 2 9 4 3 10 51 Tempting baseline; reject. Reinvents ICU, plural rules, fallback chains, and translator workflow from scratch. Save 0 days up front, lose 60+ days over the migration.

Why next-intl wins

Three structural factors put next-intl ahead of the field for Vector specifically:

  1. App Router is Vector's reality, not a wishlist item. Vector already runs the App Router (route groups, nested layouts, server components in /topology, /p/<uuid>, (user)). next-intl was designed for this from version 3 onwards and is the only library that returns translations from getTranslations() inside an RSC without forcing a 'use client' boundary. Every other library is a port from the Pages Router era and shows it.
  2. ICU MessageFormat is a non-negotiable for serious i18n. Without ICU, plurals are broken in Russian/Polish/Arabic the day you ship them. next-intl, react-intl, and LinguiJS support full ICU; raw i18next supports a subset. next-intl additionally ships icu-minify which precompiles ICU strings to AST at build time, dropping the runtime parser and putting the live JS under 1 KB.
  3. Type-safe keys catch the bug class that bites bolt-on i18n hardest. next-intl 4 generates a TypeScript declaration of every key in messages/en.json; t('some.missing.key') becomes a TS error rather than a runtime fallback to the raw key. This converts "ship a typo to production" into "ship doesn't even compile."

4. Recommended Migration Path for Vector

Eight phases. Effort sizing is consistent with the existing Fibonacci EST labels in the <stories> system: S = F2–F3, M = F5–F8, L = F13–F21, XL = needs further decomposition. Each phase lists three concrete files in the current Vector codebase that would be the first to change.

Phase 0 — Discovery and tooling (S, low risk)

  • Scope: install eslint-plugin-i18next, enable no-literal-string with mode: 'jsx-only'; add it to the existing npm run lint chain alongside lint:addressables / lint:role-literals; write a one-shot Python extraction script that walks app/**/*.tsx and emits a count + sample of literal strings per file. Add a CSV report to dev/reports/.
  • Effort: S. Risk: low. Prereq: none.
  • Files touched: package.json, .eslintrc (or eslint.config.js), new dev/scripts/scan_literal_strings.py.
  • Exit criterion: a baseline number (e.g. "Vector has 6,412 literal strings across 487 files") and a CI rule that prevents the count from growing on new PRs.

Phase 1 — Substrate (M, medium risk)

  • Scope: install next-intl@4; choose locale routing (recommended: subpath — /en/..., /fr/... — preserves caching + SEO; cookies are a tie-breaker for the locale chooser, not the source of truth); set fallback to en; create i18n/request.ts, i18n/routing.ts, messages/en.json empty stub; wire middleware. Add a thin wrapper useT(scope) that namespaces by code-relative path so call sites stay short. Generate the typed declaration via next-intl's built-in TS plugin.
  • Effort: M. Risk: medium — touches every layout's rendering path; must not break the addressables substrate (samantha._viewport.<slot>) which uses structural English keys, not display strings.
  • Prereq: Phase 0 baseline so the impact of new useT wraps is measurable.
  • Files touched: app/layout.tsx, middleware.ts, new i18n/request.ts.
  • Exit criterion: the existing app continues to render English at /en/..., type-check passes with the new generated declaration, no addressable IDs change.

Phase 2 — UI strings (static) (L, medium risk)

  • Scope: extract literal strings from app/components/** and app/(user)/** in batches by route group. Recommended cadence: one batch per sprint, each batch <500 strings, each batch behind its own PR with screenshots. Translate to English-only first; second locale (suggest French, German, or Spanish for largest pluralization-coverage payoff) lands when the catalogue is ~80% populated. Lock catalogue keys to a stable namespace per file (e.g. panel.team_flow.title); never key by content.
  • Effort: L (probably XL — will need decomposition by route group). Risk: medium — every PR touches user-visible UI.
  • Prereq: Phase 1 substrate live, Phase 0 ESLint rule blocking new literals.
  • Files touched: app/components/panel/Panel.tsx, app/store/ui_apps/ui_app_team_flow/index.tsx, every file under app/(user)/workspace-settings/.
  • Exit criterion: the literal-string baseline from Phase 0 falls below an agreed threshold (e.g. <200 remaining, all on a known carve-out list).

Phase 3 — Backend-emitted strings (M, medium risk)

  • Scope: change Go handlers to return translation keys + interpolation params instead of pre-formatted English. Audit log labels, sprint/status names, system-generated work-item titles, and error messages all switch to a uniform shape: { "key": "err.itm.deleted_archived", "params": { "title": "Sprint 12" } }. Frontend resolver lives in next-intl. Where Go must emit text directly (transactional emails, CSV exports), use github.com/nicksnyder/go-i18n/v2 with the same JSON catalogue (round-tripped from messages/en.json via a build-step transform). The existing error-codes registry (docs/c_c_error_codes.md) is already half this work — finish it.
  • Effort: M. Risk: medium — breaks any client that consumes raw error.message; requires a coordinated err.message → err.key swap on the frontend.
  • Prereq: Phase 1 substrate; Phase 2 catalogue at >50% coverage so the frontend resolver actually has the keys.
  • Files touched: backend/internal/orgdesign/handler.go, backend/internal/wsperms/handler.go, backend/internal/users/handler.go.
  • Exit criterion: zero hardcoded English strings returned in API JSON bodies; lint script proves it.

Phase 4 — DB content (user- and gadmin-authored) (L, high risk)

  • Scope: design choice between two patterns. Recommendation: sidecar tables.
PatternProsConsVector fit
JSONB column — name_i18n jsonb = {en: "...", fr: "..."} One-row read, no joins, easy to add new locale Per-locale full-text indexing is awkward, GIN index on jsonb is heavier than per-row text index, schema is opaque to ad-hoc SQL, tenant-scoped row-locks unchanged Bad — Vector needs per-locale tsvector for search and per-locale RLS predicates would become subqueries
Sidecar table — pane_help_i18n(pane_id, locale, body_html, tsv tsvector) Per-locale full-text index, clean RLS, normal joins, same pattern across every table Adds one join per read, write path is two statements (parent + i18n row) Recommended — matches the existing polymorphic FK pattern (docs/c_polymorphic_writes.md) and the entityrefs service shape
Hybrid — canonical name column for English (write-first), _i18n sidecar for translations Migration is one-way (existing rows become the en row), reads are fast for the canonical locale Still sidecar; English is privileged forever Acceptable transitional state if the migration of existing rows into the i18n sidecar is too expensive in one shot
  • Effort: L. Risk: high — schema migration on every table that holds user-facing text; affects backups, RLS, and the polymorphic FK contract.
  • Prereq: Phases 1–3 complete; gadmin tooling exists for translators to author per-locale rows.
  • Files touched: db/schema/090+_*.sql (one migration per table), backend/internal/<domain>/service.go for each service that owns translatable content, app/lib/topologyApi.ts (and equivalents) to send/receive locale-aware bodies.
  • Exit criterion: a tenant can switch locale and all gadmin-authored content (Page Help, library release severity descriptions, default custom-field labels) flips; tenant-authored content shows the canonical locale + a "not translated" badge in non-canonical locales.

Phase 5 — Formatting (S, low risk)

  • Scope: replace ad-hoc toLocaleString() / date-fns calls with next-intl's useFormatter() hook (which delegates to Intl.DateTimeFormat, Intl.NumberFormat, Intl.RelativeTimeFormat). Add an ESLint rule banning direct toLocaleString() calls in app/**. CLDR plural rules for non-trivial languages come for free with ICU MessageFormat (handled inside next-intl).
  • Effort: S. Risk: low — mostly mechanical.
  • Prereq: Phase 1.
  • Files touched: app/components/panel/PanelTime.tsx, app/lib/format.ts (new central wrapper), any chart/table cell renderer that shows a number.

Phase 6 — RTL (M, medium risk)

  • Scope: add dir attribute to <html> driven by current locale; audit app/globals.css and every theme pack in app/themes/ to replace physical properties (left, right, margin-left, padding-right) with logical equivalents (inset-inline-start, margin-inline-start, padding-inline-end); audit Canvas2D-rendered components (<DiagramCanvas>) for hardcoded LTR directional assumptions in node layout. RTL adoption is gated until at least one RTL locale ships.
  • Effort: M. Risk: medium — CSS audit will surface visual regressions across many panes; the chart catalogue must be re-screenshotted in RTL.
  • Prereq: Phase 1; commitment to ship at least one RTL language (Arabic / Hebrew).
  • Files touched: app/layout.tsx (set dir), app/globals.css, every app/themes/<pack>/theme.css.

Phase 7 — Translator workflow (M, low risk)

  • Scope: stand up Tolgee self-hosted (Apache-2.0; Docker image; sits next to the Planka container in the dev compose file). CI string-export job pushes messages/en.json to Tolgee on merge to main; CI string-import job pulls translated locales nightly into a PR. Wire Tolgee's in-context editing (Alt-click) for the staging environment so PMs can review translations against real screens. Screenshot capture runs against the existing Selenium grid (<seleniumup>) for context bundles.
  • Effort: M. Risk: low — isolated infrastructure component; no production data flows through it.
  • Prereq: Phase 2 catalogue at >80% coverage so translators have something real to translate.
  • Files touched: new docker-compose.tolgee.yml, new .github/workflows/i18n-export.yml, app/layout.tsx (Tolgee in-context SDK conditional on staging env).

Phase summary

PhaseScopeEffortRiskPrereq
0 — Discovery + toolingESLint gate, baseline scanSLow—
1 — Substratenext-intl, routing, type-safe keysMMedium0
2 — UI stringsExtract app/** literalsL (XL)Medium1
3 — Backend stringsGo returns keys + paramsMMedium1, 2
4 — DB contentSidecar _i18n tablesLHigh1, 2, 3
5 — FormattinguseFormatter(), lint toLocaleStringSLow1
6 — RTLLogical CSS, dir attrMMedium1, RTL locale committed
7 — Translator workflowTolgee + CI export/importMLow2

5. Vector-Specific Risks

5.1 Hardcoded English baked into HTML stored in DB

The Page Help system, library release severity descriptions, and any custom-field default values currently store English HTML in the database. The Phase 4 sidecar migration must back-fill an en row for every existing record before any other locale row can be written; otherwise the read-fallback chain (locale → en → raw) finds nothing. Recommended: ship the schema migration (add _i18n table, NOT NULL, FK back to parent), then a data migration that copies the existing English column into the new en row, then a code migration that switches reads through the join. Three separate PRs, not one.

5.2 Server-side audit log strings and error codes

The cross-cutting error-codes registry (docs/c_c_error_codes.md) already routes through a code library — this is half of i18n already in place. The remaining work is to ensure every error-code entry has an associated message-key entry in messages/en.json with the same code, and that the Go handlers return { "code": "ERR_XYZ", "params": {...} } — not { "message": "..." }. The reportError() call sites listed in that doc are the touch list. Audit log labels (action verbs like "archived", "created") need their own catalogue namespace, e.g. audit.action.archived, because they are reused across resource types.

5.3 <Panel> name prop vs addressables substrate

Per docs/c_c_addressables.md, every Panel/Table/Navigation has a name prop that becomes part of the addressable identifier (samantha._viewport.<slot>._kind.<name>). That name is structural — it must remain stable English (or English-like) for the addressables substrate — but Panel display titles must be translatable. The clean separation: the name prop stays the structural English key, and a new title prop (or titleKey taking a message-catalogue key) drives the visible header. Lint rule: name on Panel/Table/Navigation must be a string literal (already enforced by lint:addressables); a parallel rule must require either title or titleKey for every adopter that renders a header.

5.4 pane_help table (already shipped) needs a locale column

The pane help system stores body_html as English-only. Migration shape: new pane_help_i18n(pane_id uuid, locale text, body_html text, updated_at, PRIMARY KEY (pane_id, locale)); back-fill locale = 'en' rows from the existing body_html column; switch reads to pane_help_i18n WHERE locale IN (active, 'en') ORDER BY locale = active DESC LIMIT 1. The original body_html column on pane_help stays for one release as a compat shim, then drops in the next. The Samantha SDK help contract returns the resolved-locale body; clients never see the column, so the fallback chain is contained.

5.5 Postgres text-search and ordering

Every tsvector column today uses the implicit simple or english config; full-text search will return zero hits for the same term in German, French, or Mandarin. Two viable strategies:

  1. Per-locale tsvector columns on the sidecar table (tsv_en tsvector, tsv_fr tsvector, ...): wide table, easy queries, easy to add a new locale. Recommended.
  2. Locale-parameterised text-search config at query time: narrower table, but every read path must know the active locale and pick the matching regconfig; easier to forget.

Sort order is the other half: alphabetical sort of name in a German UI must use the German collation (ORDER BY name COLLATE "de-x-icu" with the icu extension). Postgres ICU collations are widely available but require the locale identifiers to match between front-end requests and back-end COLLATE clauses.

5.6 Worker boundary writes need an i18n-aware exemption

The lint:writer-boundary rule (docs/c_c_lint_rules.md) restricts which packages may write to which tables. The new _i18n sidecar tables must be added to the parent service's writer set (e.g. backend/internal/orgdesign/service.go writes org_nodes and org_nodes_i18n); otherwise every write becomes a boundary violation. Add _i18n-table writes to dev/registries/writer_boundary_exempt.json only as a last resort.

5.7 Form drafts (IDB) leak partial translations

Per docs/c_c_form_drafts.md, draft form state lives in IndexedDB. If a user starts a form in French, the draft contains French strings keyed against French message keys; if they switch to English mid-draft, the form must re-resolve labels but keep the user-typed values intact. The useDraft hook needs to namespace by message-key version (or strip resolved labels from drafts on save) so changing locale does not silently corrupt the draft.

5.8 Theme packs and chart components

The chart catalogue (<chart> skill output in app/components/) renders to Canvas2D and SVG. Axis labels, tooltip text, and legend entries are passed in as props today — trivial to switch to message keys. The sanitised preview random generator inside each chart hardcodes English category names (e.g. "Q1", "Q2"); these need a message-key namespace (chart.preview.q1) or a documented carve-out as "preview-only English" in the lint exempt registry.

7. Gaps

  • No measured baseline of literal-string count in Vector today — Phase 0 produces this number; until then, sizing of Phase 2 is approximate.
  • next-intl Pro tier (if any) and roadmap for Next.js 16 are not enumerated in primary docs fetched.
  • Tolgee's self-hosted scaling characteristics at >100k keys / >20 locales not benchmarked in primary sources; small-to-medium catalogues are within scope based on community reports.
  • FormatJS RSC support status is "known open issue" per primary GitHub issue #4192; final landing date not announced — conclusion that react-intl is not RSC-ready is current as of search but should be re-checked at Phase 1 commit time.
  • No primary benchmarks comparing next-intl vs i18next bundle size with identical message catalogue and ICU usage; quoted next-intl ~1 KB compiled and i18next ~15–25 KB are vendor figures, not independently audited.
  • Postgres ICU collation availability on the production Docker image (docs/c_deployment.md) needs confirmation before Phase 4; the alpine variant of the official postgres image historically ships without ICU.
  • Translation workflow for tenant-authored content (where a customer writes in their own language and other tenants of the same product consume it — or not) is intentionally out of scope of this paper; the recommendation defers that decision to a separate research task.
